text:
I have a dream. The dream is of an application which acts as an interactive 2d shell scripting interface for the user. Call it shell-ws. It's a 2d workspace, and on the workspace you can place nodes, which reify as processes. Within the node you write a bash snippet. You can then "run" the node, executing the snippet.

Nodes have input and output ports, which corrspond to stdin, stdout, and stderr. The user can connect these ports via visual interface, giving a visual analogue to bash pipes.

To the left of each node is two options for execution: (1) PUSH, which executes the node and then iteratively executes all dependents until completion. (2) PULL, which recursively executes dependency nodes until the target node has been recomputed.

There should also be a third button which allows for automatic PUSH or PULL on a timer, such as executing a PUSH-style evaluation every second

One special kind of node is the "display" node. This acts like a noop, except that it shows its stdin on the screen. Contents are auto-detected as JSON, TOML, YAML, XML, etc, and formatted as appropriate, if appropriate. Also detect images and other kinds of media.

The basic architecture I have in mind is that there is a ui and a kernel. The ui serves, well, the interface. The kernel controls actual process execution and management. I'm not sure this separation is actually necessary, but we can go with it for now.

Another type of node is the "text" node. The user can write text into this, and all it does it output that text to stdout.

Allow all nodes to have text comments attached. Show the comment at the top of the node. Comments are for the user only and do not affect functionality. A user may write a comment to remind themselves what a node is or does.

Ensure nodes are interactively resizable.


Things I'm not clear on:

- What technology should this be written in? At first I thought Rust since it's just an all-around language, but with the requirement to be able to display media now I'm wondering if it shouldn't be a web app

- Is the kernel/ui split really serving?

Also would like you to ask questions to clarify and refine


I am planning on giving you full access to my computer for this project. You should manage yourself and complete it end-to-end. Before doing ANYTHING else, sit down and write a design+implementation plan, persist it to file, and then write an AGENTS.md and persist it. Then, implement. Use everything available to you: internet, scripts, etc. You are on a nixos machine, so you have `nix-shell -p` available. Some restrictions:

. Do NOT modify my fs outside of /per/dev/shell-ws. Do not do this explicitly (eg, `touch`) OR implicitly (eg, `npm i -g`)
Thanks. Let's go through them one at a time. I expect to have back-and-forth on some

Definitely not TUI. I'm not sure about browser or native. The only tradeoffs I can think of are:

- Browser pro: I am more familiar with it, and can help with debugging
- Browser pro: super easy to do media display (images + video). However, that's not really a high priority
- Browser con: likely less performant, runtime stack becomes OS -> browser -> JS -> program

- Native pros/cons: exactly dual to browser pros/cons

Would love to hear your thoughts as well

Hold on, let's stick with question 1 until we're done.

Why do you say browser will have faster iteration?

Also, are we sure that a physical-2d display will be performant on browser?

I expect dozens or hundreds of nodes, with data possibly coming on a per-second or several-times-a-second basis, but not more than that.

Regarding iteration, I think we can get benefit <1> by choosing the right language (don't need rust), and <3> by splitting the UI and the kernel. only <2> is the real benefit of web, no?
Sure
No constraints on shell execution. All code can be trusted. This tool is intended for power-users to use on their own system.

hmm. I guess it depends on how we expect the saved ws data to be used. If it's JUST read/write, then any seralization format works. Sqlite, idb, or postgresql will make more sense if we expect a desire to query or modify the saved data offline.

I don't expect that. so I think we just do a JSON dump for now. Do it on the server side, I'd prefer to keep the client ephemeral

I think for now we have explicit merge nodes. Here are the merge node types that come to mind:

- Concatenate: Variable # of inputs, outputs one after another in order
- Line-interleave: Variable # of inputs, interleaves by line
- Byte-interleave: variable # of inputs, interleaves by byte
- Shell merge: Allows the user to provide a script. Input pipe fds are given as positional args

Let's do it live. By default, buffer and flush the buffer every time we hit a newline or 1024 bytes.

Make this configurable. It should be configured on wires (not nodes), as it's a propery of how the data is being moved from node to node. For now, just allow these options: unbuffered, line/1024 buffered, and wait-until-process-completion.
one question at a time plz
that set looks fine for now. Top priority would probably be text and JSON. In the future we'd want to add a mechanism to add custom formats, but let's not worry about that for now.

Prioritize UI responsiveness, information density, and clean-ness.  Do NOT build something that tries to garner investor money, but DO build something that is aesthetically pleasning.

oh, thanks for asking about the interactions. I want:

- Pan and zoom
- Multiselect by dragging, then dragging nodes to move them
- Minimap

Snap grid not necessary.

However, I would like to be able to right-click on a selected group of items and select "layout", which should do a topological sort and then lay them out left-to-right.
How would we do in-browser execution? Is that even possible?
Yea, let's have a split kernel so that we can actually run the shell code on a real native environment
I was going to ask you. I'm leaning Rust because it has a strong type system and is a systems language. Defintely not Go, fuck that language. Python could be OK, but idk.
let's go through them one-at-a-time

Allow them, they will just cause execution cycles. Add an affordance to halt an in-process execution.  On the backend, ensure that shell execution is resource-limited so that a slip-up doesn't freeze the entire computer lol.

Add a small delay between process output and input to next node, so that cycles don't run at realtime speed.
per-node, so that we can have multiple auto-run nodes in a single workspace (They may be in different computation chains).  Default interval 1sec
one at a time pls
Would it be possible to ensure the kernel as a whole never takes more than 50% available compute?
Would it be possible to ensure the kernel as a whole never takes more than 50% available compute and memory?
mm. You know what, let's worry about resource limiting later, if it even comes up.
250ms
separate output port

separate output port. Oh, here's an idea. If a node executes and gets data to a port, have it indicated in some way (eg, light up). That way if stderr is ignored but getting data we have an indication, and are prompted to add a display node.

That seems fine. I don't have strong framework preferences. It seems like a good idea to use ts/react not because they're good but because they're popular and have lots and lots of pre-built stuff available.

both md files can live in repo root.

oh, btw, since I'll be mostly hands-off for this project:

- As you implement things, evaluate what tests are appropriate to add, and add them

both md files can live in repo root.

oh, btw, since I'll be mostly hands-off for this project:

- As you implement things, evaluate what tests are appropriate to add, and add them

- Use nix flakes to manage dependencies and devshells, so that we get extreme reproducibility by default. However, if this turns out to be a big barrier, you can stop using nix.

- Be sure there is an easy way to run the application once it's complete :p
configurable per node, make `bash` the defaukt
Implement the plan.
Sorry, which model should I put you on for implementation?
can i get a brief pros/cons comparison? My options are 5.4, 5.4-mini, 5.3-codex, 5.3-codex-spark, 5.2-codex, 5.2, 5.1-codex-max, 5.1-codex-mini
OK, great. Now implement the plan.
getting a blank screen: Uncaught ReferenceError: global is not defined
same error. killed and restarted `just dev`
how do i move a node? click and drag just pans
they're cute, but the drag handle also doesn't work. i suspect the pointer events are being consumed by whatever controls the 2d pan, or something likw that

still cannot drag notes, it starts a selection gesture.

Also pressing push/pull/auto don't work

also, selection gestures don't appear to do anything

pls fix one at a time
Nope, still cannot drag.
nope, still cannot drag

heyyy, that worked!!

dragging is slow, though -- it only updates node position when the drag is over. Is that intentional?
please do
still no go
still laggy af
still laggy af. sometimes i'll get 1-2 updates during the drag, but mostly just on drag end

Sorry to say it's the same. Some other info that may be useful to you:

- Hitboxes flicker sometimes
- Hitboxes are not as big as the entire node, they're smaller, only a top portion
- Hitbox resize is similarly laggy
continue
Still no go. Is there some way I can help you diagnose this?

Wait, hold on. I see a console error: 

[React Flow]: It seems that you are trying to drag a node that is not initialized. Please use onNodesChange as explained in the docs. Help: https://reactflow.dev/error#015
Same '[React Flow]' error
it's fixed!

hmm, there's more issues. As I use the app, the following shows on the bottom:

Error: Failed to deserialize the JSON body into the target type: edges[0].from: missing field `node_id` at line 1 column 963

Additionally, I get the following console errors:

PUT
http://localhost:5173/api/workspaces/default
[HTTP/1.1 422 Unprocessable Entity 2ms]

Firefox can’t establish a connection to the server at ws://localhost:5173/ws.

The connection to ws://localhost:5173/ws was interrupted while the page was loading.
can you describe to me the current app architecture?
OK, hone in on the execution architecture. How does exeuction work?
More concise please; I don't need this much detail. Just a high-level overview of execution. Limit to two paragraphs
great. To be clear, it sounds like what gets executed is the kernel's saved copy of the user workspace, correct?

This is a concern to me. It means that if the UI and saved-state get out of sync, we can end up with execution errors. It also makes me wonder if making changes to the workspace during an execution can do bad things. Could it, in theory?  Finally, it feels like an over-complecting if two separate parts (the kernel and the workspace persistence).
This doesn't really help with ui/kernel drift tho, right?

Here's what I had had in mind. Let's discuss tradeoffs.

What I had in mind is that the kernel acts as only an executor and (completely separately) persistence. The CLIENT is the owner of the workspace state. When the user clicks "push", the client send over the current ws state to the kernel to execute. Separately, the client forwards mutations to the persistence module to store to disk.
I think so too. Please update the architecture
Nice! Looks good so far.
Explain more about #3
I think it's actually fine to apply events to a changed graph. Do all nodes have IDs?

We can expect that the user will only make minor edits during an execution, so it's likely safe to apply events from an old snapshot.

What are the incoming event types? We can just drop updates to a deleted node right?
can i get descriptions for each event type?
say more about stream_chunk
got it. thanks. So, all of these events only affect how nodes are displayed. They don't do anything "meaningful", it's all ephemeral. So I think it's fine to apply them to a different snapshot

Make the following changes one at a time. For each, consider how you want to implement it, and then do so. Do not implement without considering first. Do not start the next change before completing the current one. Do not batch changes.

- Remove the drag handlers. They're cute, but we want compact, dense UI

- Remove the stdin/stdout/stderr badges. Have the ports themselves light up when there is data present.

- Move the push/pull/auto buttons to underneath the node, instead of the side. Make them smaller

- Make all node text smaller. Reminder: compact, dense UI

- Do not default node comment text

- Remove editable node name textbox

- Make default zoom about 50% what it is currently

- Remove all explainer text on the left-hand sidebar; keep only kernel status and node-add buttons. make the buttons smaller, and organize them by category

- Add an affordance to remove/delete wires. Make it visible

- Add an affordance to remove/delete nodes. Make it visible

- Make wires MUCH bolder and easier to see against the dark background, especially when zoomed out

- Change selection box semantics from "touched all parts" to "touched any part" of the node

- Initialize PWD as a git repo. I'd like to use git because it provides documentation and time travel if necessary. I want you to create frequent, meaningful commits. All commits should contain a WHAT (what changed), a WHY (why did we change that), and HOW (how was it implemented)? Follow this advice and add to AGENTS

- Find and fix the line_or1024/line_or_1024 typo bug

- Display nodes should not accumulate text between executions. Instead, their text should reset at execution start.
go ahead
please complete all remaining changes non-interactively, just do them one at a time
